patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 2/2] btrfs: test qgroup deletion races with squota writes

version_history.subject: [PATCH 2/2] btrfs: test qgroup deletion races with squota writes

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds four test cases to the Btrfs file system to cover various race conditions between qgroup deletion and squota writes. The tests aim to identify potential bugs in the handling of extent ownership references (EXTENT_OWNER_REF) when a subvolume is deleted or its qgroup is destroyed. The scenarios tested include freeing an extent whose owner qgroup is gone, creating an extent while the owning subvolume is being deleted, destroying a live subvolume's qgroup with delayed refs, and destroying a qgroup belonging to a still-mounted subvolume. The patch ensures that these operations are properly handled by the Btrfs kernel to prevent underflows or transaction aborts.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch inlines the enable_quota helper function in test 301, removing unnecessary indirection and dead code. The helper is only used for simple quota enablement, which is always exercised by this test.

review_comments.summary: Expressed neutrality towards the patch and suggested reviewing it separately from the second patch in the series. Did not provide any technical feedback or opinions on the patch itself.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, May 12, 2026 at 05:43:33PM -0700, Boris Burkov wrote:
> The enable_quota helper in btrfs/301 takes a mode argument ('n', 's',
> or anything else) to switch between "no quota", "simple quota", and

> Inline the two call sites to call 'btrfs quota enable --simple'

> 
> Signed-off-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>

a minor heart attack. I thought it was a critical bug report about fstests
accidentally deleting itself while running :-D

> +++ b/tests/btrfs/301
> @@ -169,16 +169,6 @@ do_enospc_falloc()

>

> -}
> -
>  get_subvid()
>  {
>  	_btrfs_get_subvolid $SCRATCH_MNT subv
> @@ -198,7 +188,7 @@ prepare()

>  	_scratch_mount
> -	enable_quota "s"
> +	$BTRFS_UTIL_PROG quota enable --simple $SCRATCH_MNT
>  	$BTRFS_UTIL_PROG subvolume create $subv >> $seqres.full
>  	local subvid=$(get_subvid)
>  	set_subvol_limit $subvid $limit
> @@ -421,7 +411,7 @@ enable_mature()
>  	# Sync before enabling squotas to reliably *not* count the writes
>  	# we did before enabling.
>  	sync
> -	enable_quota "s"
> +	$BTRFS_UTIL_PROG quota enable --simple $SCRATCH_MNT

This change doesn't seem related to patch 2/2. I think we can review
these 2 patches separately.

For this patch, I'm fine with or without it. If the btrfs list prefers
to have it, or move it to be a common helper, I'm good with that.

Thanks,
Zorro

>  	_scratch_cycle_mount
>  	usage=$(get_subvol_usage $subvid)

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 5/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qspinlock

version_history.subject: [PATCH v6 5/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qspinlock

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a contended_release tracepoint to the qspinlock implementation, allowing for tracing of lock contention and release events. The patch uses an arch-overridable queued_spin_release() function to ensure correct behavior across all architectures, including those with custom unlock implementations. When the tracepoint is disabled, only a single NOP instruction is added to the hot path, minimizing performance impact. When enabled, the contention check, trace call, and unlock are combined in an out-of-line function to further minimize hot path impact. The patch also includes estimates of binary size increases due to this change.

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the contended_release tracepoint is not properly disabled when the lock is already unlocked, and suggested adding a check to ensure the lock is locked before tracing.

The reviewer suggested removing a phrase from the patch description that is considered unnecessary in Git change logs.

Reviewer Steven Rostedt suggested removing a 'return' statement from the code, arguing that it is unnecessary and may cause bugs in the future if modified; instead of returning immediately, the function should be called directly

The reviewer, Steven Rostedt, pointed out a redundant call to queued_spin_release() in the code and requested its removal.

On Tue,  5 May 2026 17:09:34 +0000
Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

---

Remove the ", introduced in the previous commit," That's useless in git
change logs.

---

Get rid of the "return;". What does it save you? It just makes it that you
need to duplicate the code. Even though it's a one liner, it can cause bugs
in the future if this changes. You could call the function:

  do_trace_queued_spin_release_traced(lock);

---

And then remove the duplicate call of "queued_spin_release()" here.

review_comments.author: Peter Zijlstra
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Peter Zijlstra pointed out that the patch adds unnecessary instructions to the hot path, specifically a CALL and JMP instruction in addition to the initial NOP, increasing binary size by 12-15 bytes. He also disagreed with the claim that UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK=n configurations opt against binary size optimization on x86.
review_comments.raw_body:
This is not quite accurate. You add the (5byte) NOP for the static
branch, but then you also add another 5 bytes for the CALL and at least
another 2 bytes (possibly 5) for a JMP back into the previous stream.
That is 12-15 bytes added to what was a single MOV instruction.

That is quite ludicrous.

I disagree that UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK=n opts against binary size. For x86
the unlock is smaller than a function call.


I really don't see how this is worth it.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author addressed Peter's concern about the size increase of the generated code due to the added tracepoint. The author provided detailed assembly code comparisons between the baseline and patched versions, showing that while the total size increase is 9 bytes, the executed path only adds a 2-byte NOP instruction, which has special treatment in CPU.

Author acknowledges that the original binary size increase was due to a single 2-byte NOP instruction and asks if this change alleviates the reviewer's concerns.

Author acknowledged a characterization error in the patch description regarding UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK on x86_64 and agreed to correct it
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for the feedback, Peter. This is exactly the kind of feedback I
was looking for.

I understand your concerns and initially I had exactly the same
thoughts, and after I looked into the generated code more carefully the
impact on the executed path is smaller than the total size increase
suggests.

Generated code of _raw_spin_unlock() for baseline (before the patch) is
31 bytes in total (x86_64, defconfig, GCC 11).

    3e0:  endbr64                          ; 4 bytes
    3e4:  movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; 3 bytes (unlock)
    3e7:  decl %gs:__preempt_count         ; 7 bytes
    3ee:  je   3f5                         ; 2 bytes
    3f0:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes
    3f5:  call __SCT__preempt_schedule     ; 5 bytes
    3fa:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes

Generated code of _raw_spin_unlock() with tracepoint (after the patch
applied) is 40 bytes in total.

    bc0:  endbr64                          ; 4 bytes
    bc4:  xchg %ax,%ax                     ; 2 bytes (NOP, static branch)
    bc6:  movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; 3 bytes (unlock)
    bc9:  decl %gs:__preempt_count         ; 7 bytes
    bd0:  je   bde                         ; 2 bytes
    bd2:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes
    bd7:  call queued_spin_release_traced  ; 5 bytes
    bdc:  jmp  bc9                         ; 2 bytes
    bde:  call __SCT__preempt_schedule     ; 5 bytes
    be3:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes

It is 40 bytes (+9 bytes compared to baseline, 2 bytes for NOP and 7
bytes for CALL and JMP).

But if we look at the executed path the picture is a bit different.

Baseline, in best case scenario of least number of executed
instructions.

    3e0:  endbr64                          ; 4 bytes (always executed)
    3e4:  movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; 3 bytes (unlock,
                                           ; always executed)
    3e7:  decl %gs:__preempt_count         ; 7 bytes (always executed)
    3ee:  je   3f5                         ; 2 bytes (always executed)
    3f0:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes (executed if above
                                           ; je is not taken)
                                           ; rest is not executed
    3f5:  call __SCT__preempt_schedule     ; 5 bytes
    3fa:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes

Tracepoint (again same case of least number of executed instructions).

    bc0:  endbr64                          ; 4 bytes (always executed)
    bc4:  xchg %ax,%ax                     ; 2 bytes (always executed, this is an
                                           ; only addition on the execution path).
    bc6:  movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; 3 bytes (unlock, always executed)
    bc9:  decl %gs:__preempt_count         ; 7 bytes (always executed)
    bd0:  je   bde                         ; 2 bytes (always executed)
    bd2:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes (executed if above
                                           ; je is not taken)
                                           ; rest is not executed
    bd7:  call queued_spin_release_traced  ; 5 bytes
    bdc:  jmp  bc9                         ; 2 bytes
    bde:  call __SCT__preempt_schedule     ; 5 bytes
    be3:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes

On the execution path we are getting 21 byte worth of instructions on
baseline against 23 bytes. The only addition on any executed path is the
2-byte NOP, that has a special treatment in CPU, cheap, but not entirely
free.

---

a single 2-byte NOP.

Does this change the picture for you, or is the NOP still a concern for
this path?

---

Fair point on the UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK characterization, but
UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCKis always "y" on x86_64. The inlined case only
applies to s390 (unconditionally), csky and loongarch (when
!PREEMPTION). I'll remove this, thanks.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author acknowledged a feedback about adding a comment to explain why the tracepoint is enabled only when contended_release is enabled, agreeing to make the change.

The author addressed a concern about the performance impact of tracing on x86_64, explaining that combining the trace and unlock in an out-of-line function minimizes hot path impact by avoiding callee-saved register save/restore.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for the suggestion, will do here and in other places.

[...]

---

This is the scenario the comment above the static branch describes.
Here's what it looks like in practice on x86_64 (defconfig, compiled
with GCC 11).

Current design (trace + unlock combined, with return):
  
    endbr64
    xchg %ax,%ax                     ; NOP (static branch)
    movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; unlock
    decl %gs:__preempt_count
    je   preempt
    jmp  __x86_return_thunk
    call queued_spin_release_traced  ; cold
    jmp  preempt_handling            ; cold
    call __SCT__preempt_schedule
    jmp  __x86_return_thunk

With the trace-only function (no return, unlock after the call):
  
    endbr64
    push %rbx                        ; saves callee-saved rbx (!)
    mov  %rdi,%rbx                   ; preserve lock across call (!)
    xchg %ax,%ax                     ; NOP (static branch)
    movb $0x0,(%rbx)                 ; unlock
    decl %gs:__preempt_count
    je   preempt
    pop  %rbx                        ; callee-saved restore (!)
    jmp  __x86_return_thunk
    call queued_spin_release_traced  ; cold
    jmp  unlock                      ; cold
    call __SCT__preempt_schedule
    pop  %rbx
    jmp  __x86_return_thunk

Three extra instructions marked by "!" on the hot path (push, mov, pop),
all wasted when the tracepoint is off. That's the main reason for
combining trace and unlock in the same out-of-line function.

review_comments.summary:
The reviewer noted that the contended_release tracepoint is not properly disabled when the lock is already held, and requested a check to be added before calling queued_spin_release_traced()

Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

Ah, because the return makes it into two tail calls.

I still don't like the duplication, perhaps add some more comments about

comment that this duplicate code helps the assembly.

-- Steve

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 3/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks

version_history.subject: [PATCH v6 3/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new tracepoint called contended_release to the Linux kernel's locking subsystem. The contended_release tracepoint fires when a lock is released under contention, allowing for correlation of lock hold time with waiter events by lock address. The patch modifies the slowpath unlock paths of various sleepable locks (mutex, rtmutex, semaphore, rwsem, percpu-rwsem, and RT-specific rwbase locks) to include calls to trace_contended_release(). This enables more detailed tracing and debugging of locking behavior in the kernel.

version_history.subject: [PATCH v6 0/7] locking: contended_release tracepoint instrumentation

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new tracepoint called contended_release that fires on the lock holder's side when a lock is released while there are waiters. This provides additional information such as hold time estimation and the holder's stack at release time, which can be used to measure how long a lock was held under contention. The patch also updates the behavior of reader/writer locks to fire the tracepoint for every reader releasing while a writer is waiting, not just the last reader.

He also disagreed with the assumption that UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK=n opts against binary size on x86, stating that the unlock is smaller than a function call.
review_comments.raw_body:
This is not quite accurate. You add the (5byte) NOP for the static
branch, but then you also add another 5 bytes for the CALL and at least
another 2 bytes (possibly 5) for a JMP back into the previous stream.
That is 12-15 bytes added to what was a single MOV instruction.

That is quite ludicrous.

I disagree that UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK=n opts against binary size. For x86
the unlock is smaller than a function call.


I really don't see how this is worth it.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author addressed Peter's concern about the size increase of generated code due to the patch, explaining that while the total size is larger, the executed path only adds a 2-byte NOP instruction, which has a special treatment in CPU and is not entirely free.

Author asks if a single 2-byte NOP is still a concern in this path after their previous changes

Author acknowledged a concern about the UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK characterization being specific to certain architectures and agreed to remove it.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for the feedback, Peter. This is exactly the kind of feedback I
was looking for.

I understand your concerns and initially I had exactly the same
thoughts, and after I looked into the generated code more carefully the
impact on the executed path is smaller than the total size increase
suggests.

Generated code of _raw_spin_unlock() for baseline (before the patch) is
31 bytes in total (x86_64, defconfig, GCC 11).

    3e0:  endbr64                          ; 4 bytes
    3e4:  movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; 3 bytes (unlock)
    3e7:  decl %gs:__preempt_count         ; 7 bytes
    3ee:  je   3f5                         ; 2 bytes
    3f0:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes
    3f5:  call __SCT__preempt_schedule     ; 5 bytes
    3fa:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes

Generated code of _raw_spin_unlock() with tracepoint (after the patch
applied) is 40 bytes in total.

    bc0:  endbr64                          ; 4 bytes
    bc4:  xchg %ax,%ax                     ; 2 bytes (NOP, static branch)
    bc6:  movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; 3 bytes (unlock)
    bc9:  decl %gs:__preempt_count         ; 7 bytes
    bd0:  je   bde                         ; 2 bytes
    bd2:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes
    bd7:  call queued_spin_release_traced  ; 5 bytes
    bdc:  jmp  bc9                         ; 2 bytes
    bde:  call __SCT__preempt_schedule     ; 5 bytes
    be3:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes

It is 40 bytes (+9 bytes compared to baseline, 2 bytes for NOP and 7
bytes for CALL and JMP).

But if we look at the executed path the picture is a bit different.

Baseline, in best case scenario of least number of executed
instructions.

    3e0:  endbr64                          ; 4 bytes (always executed)
    3e4:  movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; 3 bytes (unlock,
                                           ; always executed)
    3e7:  decl %gs:__preempt_count         ; 7 bytes (always executed)
    3ee:  je   3f5                         ; 2 bytes (always executed)
    3f0:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes (executed if above
                                           ; je is not taken)
                                           ; rest is not executed
    3f5:  call __SCT__preempt_schedule     ; 5 bytes
    3fa:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes

Tracepoint (again same case of least number of executed instructions).

    bc0:  endbr64                          ; 4 bytes (always executed)
    bc4:  xchg %ax,%ax                     ; 2 bytes (always executed, this is an
                                           ; only addition on the execution path).
    bc6:  movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; 3 bytes (unlock, always executed)
    bc9:  decl %gs:__preempt_count         ; 7 bytes (always executed)
    bd0:  je   bde                         ; 2 bytes (always executed)
    bd2:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes (executed if above
                                           ; je is not taken)
                                           ; rest is not executed
    bd7:  call queued_spin_release_traced  ; 5 bytes
    bdc:  jmp  bc9                         ; 2 bytes
    bde:  call __SCT__preempt_schedule     ; 5 bytes
    be3:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes

On the execution path we are getting 21 byte worth of instructions on
baseline against 23 bytes. The only addition on any executed path is the
2-byte NOP, that has a special treatment in CPU, cheap, but not entirely
free.

---

a single 2-byte NOP.

Does this change the picture for you, or is the NOP still a concern for
this path?

---

Fair point on the UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK characterization, but
UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCKis always "y" on x86_64. The inlined case only
applies to s390 (unconditionally), csky and loongarch (when
!PREEMPTION). I'll remove this, thanks.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author acknowledged a suggestion to add a comment explaining why the contended_release tracepoint fires for every reader releasing while a writer is waiting, not just the last reader.

The author addressed Steven Rostedt's concern about the performance impact of using a separate function for the contended_release tracepoint, explaining that three extra instructions on the hot path are wasted when the tracepoint is off and justifying combining the trace and unlock in the same out-of-line function.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for the suggestion, will do here and in other places.

[...]

---

This is the scenario the comment above the static branch describes.
Here's what it looks like in practice on x86_64 (defconfig, compiled
with GCC 11).

Current design (trace + unlock combined, with return):
  
    endbr64
    xchg %ax,%ax                     ; NOP (static branch)
    movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; unlock
    decl %gs:__preempt_count
    je   preempt
    jmp  __x86_return_thunk
    call queued_spin_release_traced  ; cold
    jmp  preempt_handling            ; cold
    call __SCT__preempt_schedule
    jmp  __x86_return_thunk

With the trace-only function (no return, unlock after the call):
  
    endbr64
    push %rbx                        ; saves callee-saved rbx (!)
    mov  %rdi,%rbx                   ; preserve lock across call (!)
    xchg %ax,%ax                     ; NOP (static branch)
    movb $0x0,(%rbx)                 ; unlock
    decl %gs:__preempt_count
    je   preempt
    pop  %rbx                        ; callee-saved restore (!)
    jmp  __x86_return_thunk
    call queued_spin_release_traced  ; cold
    jmp  unlock                      ; cold
    call __SCT__preempt_schedule
    pop  %rbx
    jmp  __x86_return_thunk

Three extra instructions marked by "!" on the hot path (push, mov, pop),
all wasted when the tracepoint is off. That's the main reason for
combining trace and unlock in the same out-of-line function.

review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, May 14, 2026 at 02:00:31PM -0400, Michael S. Tsirkin wrote:
> On Thu, May 14, 2026 at 09:49:33AM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:
> 
> There are calls with no __GFP_ZERO but they do not allocate userspace pages.
> 
>   - drm_pagemap.c: GFP_HIGHUSER -- no zero. But this is a DRM device
>     page migration, the page content is preserved from the source.
> 
>   - test_hmm.c: GFP_HIGHUSER_MOVABLE -- no zero. Test driver, pages get
>     content from device.
> 
>   - mm/ksm.c: GFP_HIGHUSER_MOVABLE -- no zero. KSM merges identical
>     pages, content comes from the source page (copy).
> 
>   - mm/memory.c new_folio = GFP_HIGHUSER_MOVABLE
>     - no zero. This is CoW, content is copied from old page.
> 
>   - mm/userfaultfd.c: GFP_HIGHUSER_MOVABLE - no zero. Content comes from userspace via userfaultfd.
> 
>   - arm64/fault.c: __GFP_ZEROTAGS not __GFP_ZERO. MTE tag zeroing, not page zeroing. Page is zeroed separately.
> 

Right, so in all of these cases, it would be just as correct to pass
USER_ADDR_NONE I imagine :]

i.e. the user address is irrelevant, and the caller is responsible for
     sanitization before return if it's relevant.

Otherwise, passing (user_addr != -1) the buddy takes care of it for you.

Just an obvious security bonus to all of this, but by no means a
requirement for your set.  Just an observation.

> 
> I'd do this on top if possible.
>

Yeah reasonable.

~Gregory

patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch addresses a regression introduced in the conversion of NLMv3 server-side procedures to xdrgen, where the nlm4svc_lookup_file function was not properly zeroing the remainder of the file handle buffer after copying only the necessary bytes. This caused non-deterministic hash values for file handles shorter than 32 bytes, leading to lock state lookup failures. The patch adds a memset operation to ensure the entire buffer is zeroed before use.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new ioctl, UFFDIO_SET_MODE, which allows a VMM to toggle between sync and async RWP modes at runtime without re-registering the userfaultfd. The ioctl takes an enable/disable bitmask of UFFD_FEATURE_* flags, with only UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC being toggleable today. When switching to async mode, pending sync waiters are woken so they retry and auto-resolve under the new mode. This patch also includes changes to ensure that readers of ctx->features are properly synchronized with the RMW operation.

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 11/14] userfaultfd: add UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC for async fault resolution

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new feature to the Linux userfaultfd system, called UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC. This feature allows for asynchronous resolution of read-write page faults, which is useful for working-set tracking in virtual machines. The kernel can now restore access to pages in-place without blocking the faulting thread, and the VMM can reconstruct the access pattern after the fact via PAGEMAP_SCAN. Additionally, this patch upgrades writable private anon PTEs when necessary to prevent COW-faults on re-access of RWP'd writable pages.

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 09/14] mm/userfaultfd: add RWP fault delivery and expose UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds support for read-write protection (RWP) in the Linux kernel's userfaultfd feature. It introduces a new mode, UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP, which allows userspace to register RWP-protected pages and receive notifications when access attempts are made on these pages. The patch also updates various fault handling functions to deliver RWP-related messages through the userfaultfd interface. This enables userspace to implement read-write protection policies for specific memory regions. The changes work with anonymous, shared memory, and hugetlb memory types. The patch exposes a new UAPI (UFFD_FEATURE_RWP) that allows userspace to query whether RWP is supported on their system.

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 05/14] mm: add MM_CP_UFFD_RWP change_protection() flag

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new change_protection() flag, MM_CP_UFFD_RWP, which is used by userfaultfd (UFFD) to implement read-write protection. The flag installs a PAGE_NONE PTE with the uffd bit set, distinguishing it from other types of faults. This allows UFFD to protect pages from being accessed without its permission. The patch also introduces a new resolve flag, MM_CP_UFFD_RWP_RESOLVE, which restores the original page protection and clears the uffd bit. The changes are applied to various memory management functions, including change_pte_range(), change_huge_pmd(), and hugetlb_change_protection(). This patch is part of a larger series that aims to improve UFFD's functionality and security.

review_comments.author: Mike Rapoport
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the patch without any specific comments or concerns.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, May 08, 2026 at 04:55:19PM +0100, Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) wrote:
> Three mm paths outside the fault handler gate on the uffd PTE bit
> today: khugepaged (skip collapse on ranges carrying markers), rmap
> (cap unmap batching), and GUP (force a fault through
> gup_can_follow_protnone). Extend each to treat VM_UFFD_RWP the same
> as VM_UFFD_WP; otherwise per-PTE RWP state is silently destroyed or
> bypassed.
> 
> khugepaged: try_collapse_pte_mapped_thp() and
> file_backed_vma_is_retractable() already refuse to collapse or
> retract page tables on ranges carrying the uffd PTE bit. Broaden the
> VMA predicate from userfaultfd_wp() to userfaultfd_protected() so
> VM_UFFD_RWP ranges get the same protection. hpage_collapse_scan_pmd()
> needs no change \u2014 its existing pte_uffd() check already catches an
> RWP PTE because it carries the uffd bit.
> 
> rmap: folio_unmap_pte_batch() caps batching at 1 for VM_UFFD_RWP so
> the restore path handles each PTE with its own marker.
> 
> GUP: gup_can_follow_protnone() forces a fault on VM_UFFD_RWP VMAs
> regardless of FOLL_HONOR_NUMA_FAULT. RWP uses protnone as an
> access-tracking marker, not for NUMA hinting, so any GUP \u2014 read or
> write \u2014 must go through the userfaultfd fault path.
>

> Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-6

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch decouples the protnone helpers from CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING, making them available independently of NUMA balancing. The capability to detect present-but-inaccessible page table entries is useful beyond NUMA balancing and is now supported by six architectures (x86_64, arm64, powerpc, s390, riscv, loongarch). The patch introduces a new configuration option CONFIG_ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE, which is selected by these architectures. This change does not introduce any functional changes but makes the protnone infrastructure more flexible and reusable.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for tracking the working set of VM guest memory using userfaultfd, allowing a VMM to identify cold pages and evict them to tiered or remote storage. The patches address various issues and feedback from reviewers, including decoupling protection none handling, renaming PTE bit macros and accessors, and preserving RWP markers during copy-on-fork operations. The series also includes self-tests for the new functionality.

review_comments.author: Kiryl (Meta) (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the decoupling of protnone PTE infrastructure from NUMA balancing by introducing CONFIG_ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE, which is selected by six architectures and makes the infrastructure available independently of NUMA balancing. The author confirmed that no functional change was made and the same set of architectures continues to have working protnone support.
review_comments.raw_body:
pte_protnone() and pmd_protnone() detect present-but-inaccessible page
table entries. This capability is useful beyond NUMA balancing -- for
example, userfaultfd working set tracking uses protnone PTEs to track
page access without unmapping pages.

Introduce CONFIG_ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE to decouple the protnone PTE
infrastructure from CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING. The six architectures that
support protnone PTEs (x86_64, arm64, powerpc, s390, riscv, loongarch)
now select this option, and CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING depends on it.

No functional change -- the same set of architectures continues to have
working protnone support, but the infrastructure is now available
independently of NUMA balancing.

review_comments.author: Kiryl (Meta) (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a feedback about the naming convention of userfaultfd PTE bit macros, specifically that they should be renamed to reflect that they are used for tracking by userfaultfd, not just write protection or NUMA hinting. The author agrees with this change and has made the necessary mechanical renames in the code.

review_comments.author: Kiryl (Meta) (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing feedback about the naming of uffd-wp PTE bit macros, which are being renamed to reflect their new meaning depending on the VMA flag (WP or RWP). The author confirms that this is a pure mechanical rename with no behavior change.

review_comments.author: Kiryl (Meta) (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the implementation of read-write protection (RWP) in userfaultfd, explaining that RWP marks ranges by combining PAGE_NONE with the uffd PTE bit and that nothing sets the flag yet.

review_comments.author: Kiryl (Meta) (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern that the uffd PTE bit must survive any kernel path that rewrites a PTE on a VM_UFFD_RWP VMA, otherwise the marker carrying PAGE_NONE semantics is silently dropped and the next access leaks past RWP tracking. The author rewired preservation through every path that rewrites a VM_UFFD_RWP PTE, including swap, device-exclusive, migration, fork, mprotect(), and copy operations.

review_comments.author: Kiryl (Meta) (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the interaction between UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and re-registration of already-registered ranges, explaining that re-registering without dropping modes that install per-PTE markers (currently WP or RWP) is rejected with -EBUSY to prevent stranding markers. The author confirmed this behavior aligns with older kernels.

review_comments.author: Kiryl (Meta) (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed the concern that RWP (read-write protection) tracking requires both PROT_NONE support and the uffd-wp PTE bit, which may not be available on all architectures. The author explained that they have added a check to mask out UFFD_FEATURE_RWP at UFFDIO_API time when PROT_NONE is not available or VM_UFFD_RWP aliases VM_NONE (32-bit), ensuring that userspace never sees an advertised-but-broken feature.
sentiment_signals: added a check to mask out UFFD_FEATURE_RWP
review_comments.raw_body:
Wire the fault side of read-write protection tracking and turn the
userspace interface on.

An RWP-protected PTE is PAGE_NONE with the uffd bit set. The
PROT_NONE triggers a fault on any access; the uffd bit distinguishes
it from plain mprotect(PROT_NONE) or NUMA hinting.

Fault dispatch, per level:

  PTE     handle_pte_fault()    -> do_uffd_rwp()
  PMD     __handle_mm_fault()   -> do_huge_pmd_uffd_rwp()
  hugetlb hugetlb_fault()       -> hugetlb_handle_userfault()

The RWP branches gate on userfaultfd_pte_rwp() / userfaultfd_huge_pmd_rwp()
(VM_UFFD_RWP plus the uffd bit) and fall through to do_numa_page() /
do_huge_pmd_numa_page() otherwise. Each delivers a
UFFD_PAGEFAULT_FLAG_RWP message through handle_userfault(); the handler
resolves it with UFFDIO_RWPROTECT clearing MODE_RWP.

userfaultfd_must_wait() and userfaultfd_huge_must_wait() add matching
protnone+uffd waiters so sync-mode fault handlers block correctly.

Expose the UAPI:

  UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP   -> UFFD_API_REGISTER_MODES
  UFFD_FEATURE_RWP           -> UFFD_API_FEATURES
  _UFFDIO_RWPROTECT          -> UFFD_API_RANGE_IOCTLS
                                UFFD_API_RANGE_IOCTLS_BASIC

UFFD_FEATURE_RWP is masked out at UFFDIO_API time when PROT_NONE is
not available or VM_UFFD_RWP aliases VM_NONE (32-bit), so userspace
never sees an advertised-but-broken feature.

Works on anonymous, shmem, and hugetlb memory.

Signed-off-by: Kiryl Shutsemau <kas@kernel.org>
Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-6

 	if (!huge_pte_write(pte) && (reason & VM_UFFD_WP))
 		return true;
+	/*
+	 * PTE is still RW-protected (protnone with uffd bit), wait for
+	 * resolution. Plain PROT_NONE without the marker is not an RWP fault.
+	 */
+	if (pte_protnone(pte) && huge_pte_uffd(pte) && (reason & VM_UFFD_RWP))
+		return true;
 
 	return false;
 }
@@ -321,8 +327,14 @@ static inline bool userfaultfd_must_wait(struct userfaultfd_ctx *ctx,
 	if (!pmd_present(_pmd))
 		return false;

-		return !pmd_write(_pmd) && (reason & VM_UFFD_WP);
+	if (pmd_trans_huge(_pmd)) {
+		if (!pmd_write(_pmd) && (reason & VM_UFFD_WP))
+			return true;
+		if (pmd_protnone(_pmd) && pmd_uffd(_pmd) &&
+		    (reason & VM_UFFD_RWP))
+			return true;
+		return false;
+	}
 
 	pte = pte_offset_map(pmd, address);
 	if (!pte)
@@ -347,6 +359,14 @@ static inline bool userfaultfd_must_wait(struct userfaultfd_ctx *ctx,
 	 */
 	if (!pte_write(ptent) && (reason & VM_UFFD_WP))
 		goto out;
+	/*
+	 * PTE is still RW-protected (protnone with uffd bit), wait for
+	 * userspace to resolve. Plain PROT_NONE without the marker is not
+	 * an RWP fault.
+	 */
+	if (pte_protnone(ptent) && pte_uffd(ptent) &&
+	    (reason & VM_UFFD_RWP))
+		goto out;
 
 	ret = false;
 out:
@@ -2086,6 +2106,15 @@ static int userfaultfd_api(struct userfaultfd_ctx *ctx,
 		uffdio_api.features &= ~UFFD_FEATURE_WP_UNPOPULATED;
 		uffdio_api.features &= ~UFFD_FEATURE_WP_ASYNC;
 	}
+	/*
+	 * RWP needs both PROT_NONE support and the uffd-wp PTE bit. The
+	 * VM_UFFD_RWP check covers compile-time unavailability; the
+	 * pgtable_supports_uffd() check covers runtime (e.g. riscv
+	 * without the SVRSW60T59B extension) where the PTE bit is declared
+	 * but not actually usable.
+	 */
+	if (VM_UFFD_RWP == VM_NONE || !pgtable_supports_uffd())
+		uffdio_api.features &= ~UFFD_FEATURE_RWP;
 
 	ret = -EINVAL;
 	if (features & ~uffdio_api.features)

On Fri, May 08, 2026 at 10:32:20AM -0700, Andrew Morton wrote:
> On Fri,  8 May 2026 16:55:12 +0100 "Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta)" <kas@kernel.org> wrote:
> 
> > This series adds userfaultfd support for tracking the working set of
> > VM guest memory, so a VMM can identify cold pages and evict them to
> > tiered or remote storage.
> > 
> > v1: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260427114607.4068647-1-kas@kernel.org/
> 
> Thanks.  I'll duck v2 for now, await more review.

Sure.

> > Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-6
> 
> For my education, and perhaps for others: can you please explain how
> you used Claude in the preparation of this series?

I'm no expert by any means, but here's how I used it here.

For this particular project there was quite a bit of path-finding.
I had a phase where I bounced ideas off Claude. It helped me
understand the problem space better and formulate possible solutions.
Rubber ducking on steroids.

Once it's clear _what_ to do, we formulate a plan on _how_. It also
involves back and forth.

Once the plan was done, I gave the go-ahead on executing it.

Userfaultfd already had a test suite, and it was extended to cover the new
functionality. I have some scripts to build the kernel and run it in a VM.
Claude knows how to use them, so at the end of plan execution I had a
functional feature.

Then the review phase. The most time-consuming and draining part.
I carefully reviewed all patches.

At this stage I use Claude as an editor.

Some of the changes I asked for required substantial rework of the whole
patchset, and I had to start the review from scratch. A good test suite and
build-test harness help to keep the whole thing from falling apart.

It took me quite a few review rounds before I was happy with the result.
Maybe between 8 and 10. I think better instructions can cut this number down.

And I need to rethink how I do the review. Reading the git log in
parallel with examining the code in the editor and giving instructions to
Claude is not very ergonomic. There's room for improvement.

Once I was happy with the patchset to give it Signed-off-by, I ran it
through Chris' review prompts several times, addressing the issues.

I hope it is helpful. I would also be glad if other folks shared their
workflow. There is probably a better way to achieve the same result.
I am new to the game.

-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

> Preparatory patch for userfaultfd read-write protection (RWP). RWP
> extends userfaultfd protection from plain write-protection (WP) to
> full read-write protection: accesses to an RWP-protected range --
> reads as well as writes -- trap through userfaultfd.

> the flag is only meaningful when both primitives exist. A new
> CONFIG_USERFAULTFD_RWP Kconfig symbol auto-selects when CONFIG_64BIT,
> CONFIG_ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE, and CONFIG_HAVE_ARCH_USERFAULTFD_WP
> are all set; call sites that gate on the flag depend on the symbol.
> Elsewhere VM_UFFD_RWP aliases VM_NONE and every downstream check
> folds to dead code.
>

On Fri, May 08, 2026 at 04:55:24PM +0100, Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) wrote:
> Add an ioctl to toggle async mode at runtime without re-registering
> the userfaultfd. This allows a VMM to switch between sync and async
> RWP modes on-the-fly -- for example, starting in async mode for
> working set scanning, then switching to sync mode to intercept faults
> during page eviction.
> 
> UFFDIO_SET_MODE takes an enable/disable bitmask of UFFD_FEATURE_*
> flags. Only UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC is toggleable today; the ioctl
> rejects any other bit with -EINVAL. Enabling RWP_ASYNC also requires
> RWP to have been negotiated at UFFDIO_API time, mirroring the
> UFFDIO_API invariant.
> 
> Fault-path readers of ctx->features run under mmap_read_lock or a
> per-VMA lock; the RMW takes mmap_write_lock and calls
> vma_start_write() on every UFFD-armed VMA, so those readers are fully
> excluded. userfaultfd_show_fdinfo(), however, reads ctx->features
> without any lock, so the RMW is written as a single WRITE_ONCE and
> fdinfo reads it with READ_ONCE. That keeps the lockless observer from
> seeing a mid-RMW intermediate and removes the audit burden when new
> toggleable bits are added later.
> 
> When switching to async, pending sync waiters are woken so they retry
> and auto-resolve under the new mode.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) <kas@kernel.org>
> Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-6

> +Read-Write Protection
> +---------------------
> +
> +``UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP`` enables read-write protection tracking on a
> +memory range. It is similar to (but faster than) ``mprotect(PROT_NONE)``
> +combined with a signal handler; unlike ``mprotect(PROT_NONE)``, RWP only
> +traps accesses to *present* PTEs, so accesses to unpopulated addresses in a
> +protected range fall through to the normal missing-page path. It uses the
> +PROT_NONE hinting mechanism (same as NUMA balancing) to make pages
> +inaccessible while keeping them resident in memory. Works on anonymous,
> +shmem, and hugetlbfs memory.

> +**Scope of protection:**
> +
> +RWP protection is a property of *present* PTEs. ``UFFDIO_RWPROTECT`` only
> +affects entries that are already populated. Unpopulated addresses within
> +the range remain unpopulated; when first accessed they fault through the

> +**Fault Handling:**
> +
> +When a protected page is accessed:
> +
> +- **Sync mode** (default): The faulting thread blocks and a
> +  ``UFFD_PAGEFAULT_FLAG_RWP`` message is delivered to the userfaultfd

> +  (clearing ``MODE_RWP``), which restores the PTE permissions and wakes
> +  the faulting thread.
> +
> +- **Async mode** (``UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC``): The kernel automatically
> +  restores PTE permissions and the thread continues without blocking. No
> +  message is delivered to the handler.

> +**Runtime Mode Switching:**

> +``UFFDIO_SET_MODE`` toggles ``UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC`` at runtime, allowing

> +ensure all in-flight faults complete before the mode change takes effect.

> +**Cold Page Detection with PAGEMAP_SCAN:**

> +**VMM Working Set Tracking Workflow:**
> +
> +A typical VMM lifecycle for cold page eviction to tiered storage. Two
> +mappings of the same shmem (or hugetlbfs) file are used: ``guest_mem`` is

> +    /* One-time setup */

> +    io_mem    = mmap(NULL, guest_size, PROT_READ | PROT_WRITE,

> +
> +    uffd = userfaultfd(O_CLOEXEC | O_NONBLOCK);

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] x86/entry: Read CR2 in asm entry stub to redcue NMI clobbering window

version_history.subject: [PATCH] x86/entry: Read CR2 in asm entry stub to redcue NMI clobbering window

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch reduces the window where an NMI can clobber the CR2 register by reading it immediately after error_entry in the asm entry stub, rather than in the C handler. This is done by moving the read_cr2() function into the asm entry stub and passing the address as a third argument to exc_page_fault(). The patch also updates various files to use the new idtentry_pf macro and passes the CR2 value correctly to the C handler. This change reduces the NMI/CR2 race window by 90%.

review_comments.author: Dave Hansen
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Dave Hansen questioned whether the added assembly code is worth it to reduce but not eliminate a race condition for non-FRED systems, asking about the actual impact on users.
review_comments.raw_body:
I'm all for using the FRED data. That's a no-brainer.

But I'm not sure the assembly and plumbing is worth it just to _reduce_
but not eliminate a race for non-FRED systems.

What's the actual end-user-visible fallout from the race? Don't we just
see the KASAN fault, skip it, IRET from the #PF and repeat the #PF?

review_comments.author: Rik Riel (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledges a potential issue in production systems due to an NMI/CR2 race window, but lacks information on symptoms and frequency.
review_comments.raw_body:
That's a good question.

I have only "seen" this in syzkaller, so I don't know
what the symptoms would look like if a regular system
hit this race window.

I would not be surprised if we occasionally hit this
in production, but given that I don't know what the
symptoms would look like, I have no idea how often.


-- 
All Rights Reversed.

review_comments.author: Dave Hansen
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Dave Hansen noted that the idtentry_body_pf macro does not handle the case where the error_entry function is already using the %rdx register, which could lead to a register clobbering issue.

Reviewer Dave Hansen expressed concern that the patch does not provide a robust solution for the problem of NMI clobbering CR2, and suggested focusing on implementing the FRED bits instead.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 5/13/26 09:43, Rik van Riel wrote:
...

---

I don't think there's a concrete enough problem being solved here to
justify adding complexity to the entry code. Especially since it's not
even a robust solution for the problem.

The FRED thing is simple, robust, and doesn't affect the actual (nasty)
entry assembly.

I think we should do the FRED bits and continue to ignore the pre-FRED
problem.

review_comments.author: Andrew Cooper
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that CR2 is not the only register affected by the NMI/CR2 race window, and that DR6 and XFD_ERR are also problematic. They emphasized that preserving these registers requires the NMI handler to do so in assembly before entering C.
review_comments.raw_body:
Well, corrupting CR2 is a real problem.

The very best case is that fixing the pagefault at the wrong CR2 is
benign, then rerun the faulting instruction having not fixed it up, and
get a repeat fault which this time wins the race with the NMI and
doesn't corrupt CR2, and gets fixed up properly.

It's not just CR2 which is a problem; DR6 and XFD_ERR. (the other
registers which have automatic side effects from exception delivery).

Really, the NMI handler needs to preserve the registers it potentially
clobbers, and that still requires the NMI handler doing it in assembly
before entering C.

~Andrew

review_comments.raw_body:
Yup, totally agree.

It's not rocket science, and it's what we do for CR3 because of PTI, but
it's also no fun. So I didn't want to suggest it.

I mean, we've lived with this behavior _forever_, right? So how bad
could it be?

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] input/evdev: move kill_fasync() outside buffer_lock to fix SOFTIRQ deadlock

version_history.subject: [PATCH] input/evdev: move kill_fasync() outside buffer_lock to fix SOFTIRQ deadlock

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a potential deadlock in the Linux kernel's input/evdev driver by moving the call to kill_fasync() outside of the buffer_lock spinlock. The buffer_lock is SOFTIRQ-safe, but kill_fasync() acquires a SOFTIRQ-unsafe lock, creating a potential ordering violation that can lead to a deadlock. By moving the kill_fasync() call after the buffer_lock is released, the patch resolves this issue and prevents the deadlock from occurring.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Torokhov
review_comments.summary:
reviewer noted that the patch does not actually fix the SOFTIRQ deadlock because it still acquires fa_lock while holding buffer_lock in evdev_pass_values(), and requested the lock be dropped before calling wake_up_interruptible_poll()

reviewer questioned whether the patch actually fixes the issue, pointing out that the code runs while holding input->event_lock with IRQs disabled
sentiment_signals: questioning the effectiveness of the fix
review_comments.raw_body:

Does this really fix anything? This code is running holding
input->event_lock with IRQs off...

Thanks.

review_comments.author: Rik Riel (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that the SOFTIRQ deadlock issue is caused by kill_fasync() acquiring a SOFTIRQ-unsafe lock, and agreed to restructure in v2.

The author acknowledges that the original patch does not fix the SOFTIRQ deadlock issue, and is seeking feedback on whether a suggested alternative (Sashiko) would work or if there's a better approach.
